era_context: The Roman Empire under Trajan (98–117 CE) at its territorial zenith following the conquest of Dacia (106 CE). The Parthian Empire controls Mesopotamia and the Levant. In China, the Han Dynasty (206 BCE–220 CE) is in its final century of stability under the Eastern Han. India sees the decline of the Indo-Greek kingdoms and the rise of the Satavahana Empire in the Deccan. This is a period of relative peace and administrative consolidation across the major empires.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Northern Europe beyond the Rhine-Danube frontier (sparse archaeological record)

events.title: Trajan's Dacian Wars conclude; Dacia becomes Roman province
events.description: Emperor Trajan completes the conquest of Dacia, begun in 101 CE, establishing it as a Roman province east of the Danube. This represents the empire's greatest territorial expansion. The conquest is documented on Trajan's Column in Rome and in later historical accounts. The region becomes heavily Romanized and serves as a buffer against Parthian and steppe incursions.
events.certainty_note: The conquest is well-documented in contemporary monumental evidence and later historiography. Dating to 106 CE is secure; the consolidation and administrative establishment continued through 110 CE.

events.title: Han Dynasty administrative reforms under Emperor Andi
events.description: Emperor Andi of Han (r. 106–125 CE) consolidates power following the regency of Empress Deng. Administrative reforms strengthen central authority and reduce the power of regional warlords. The period sees relative stability in the empire, though eunuch influence at court begins to increase, a trend that will destabilize the dynasty in later decades.

sources.name: Book of Han (Hanshu) and Later Han History (Hou Hanshu)

events.certainty_note: The broad outlines of Andi's reign and Empress Deng's regency are well-established in Chinese historiography, though specific administrative measures in 110 CE are not always precisely dated.

events.title: Parthian-Roman tensions over Armenia and Mesopotamia
events.description: Following Trajan's consolidation of Dacia, tensions rise between Rome and Parthia over control of Armenia and Mesopotamia. These regions remain contested between the two superpowers. No major military engagement occurs in 110 CE, but diplomatic and strategic maneuvering intensifies, setting the stage for future conflicts.
events.certainty_note: The broader pattern of Rome-Parthia rivalry is well-documented; specific events in 110 CE are inferred from the trajectory of relations rather than contemporary records.

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional narrative of Trajan as an unambiguously successful conqueror has been complicated by modern scholarship emphasizing the economic and military costs of the Dacian Wars and the difficulty of holding the province long-term (Dacia was abandoned in 271 CE). The 'golden age' framing of Trajan's reign obscures internal strains, including growing eunuch influence in the Eastern Han and the fragility of Parthian-Roman equilibrium.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 110 CE is relatively rich for the Roman and Chinese empires, drawing on contemporary monumental evidence, near-contemporary chronicles, and later historiographic synthesis. The Parthian perspective is underrepresented, relying largely on Greek and Roman sources. Sub-Saharan Africa, northern Europe, and Southeast Asia are virtually absent from the record.

graph_edges.note: Trajan's consolidation of Dacia and eastern frontier strengthens his position for the Parthian War
Andi's reign continues the Eastern Han administrative tradition
